data.title: Who Will Benefit From Deregulation?
data.description:
By Christian Floro, CFA, CMT, Market Strategist and Kiara Cao, Analyst, Multi-Asset Research

At a time when the U.S. economy is grappling with rising trade barriers and policy uncertainty, one area of renewed momentum may offer a counterweight: deregulation. While early moves by the Trump administration in its second term—particularly around trade and government restructuring—have unsettled markets and weighed on growth expectations, the policy agenda appears to be shifting. Investors frustrated by the initial sequencing of growth-damaging measures may now find relief as more pro-growth initiatives begin to take shape.

Among these, deregulation stands out. It played a central role during Trump’s first term, when the administration succeeded in curbing the growth of new regulatory costs to levels not seen in decades. When combined with the 2017 tax cuts, that deregulatory push delivered an estimated $1.5 trillion boost to the U.S. economy. A similar strategy today—particularly if paired with an extension of those earlier tax cuts—could help restore business confidence and revive capital expenditure plans delayed by trade tensions and broader policy uncertainty.

Source: American Action Forum, Principal Asset Management. Data as of June 11, 2025.

The deregulatory framework

While there is still uncertainty around how the Trump administration will pursue its deregulatory agenda—whether primarily through executive action or existing legal mechanisms—the Congressional Review Act (CRA) offers an early window into its priorities.

The CRA, a tool the administration used extensively in its first term, allows Congress to overturn recently finalized regulations with a simple majority vote. Under the law, agencies must submit new rules to Congress, creating a formal list of regulations eligible for review.

For investors, this list serves as a useful guide. It offers a relatively comprehensive snapshot of which sectors may be most affected by regulatory rollbacks. By tracking which regulations are being flagged for CRA review, market participants can better anticipate the administration’s focus areas—and where the most impactful deregulatory efforts may be headed.

Key impacted sectors

Among the roughly 80 regulations submitted through the CRA for review, the bulk of these rules are related to energy and the environment, followed by financials and healthcare.

Source: Jefferies, Principal Asset Management. Data as of May 14, 2025.

Sectors that stand to potentially benefit the most are traditional energy, industrials, and financials. Relaxing or repealing restrictions on oil & gas drilling and extraction, together with a reduction of compliance costs, is likely to help traditional energy companies and industrial firms focused on capital goods. Meanwhile, an easing of financial regulations, including lower capital or liquidity requirements, or a relaxation of M&A regulations, is likely to boost profitability within the financial sector.

On the other hand, sectors that may be most negatively impacted could be healthcare and industries associated with the environment. Changes to public healthcare subsidies related to either Medicaid or Medicare could negatively impact patient demand, weighing on hospital spending. Meanwhile, cuts to regulations governing clean energy are likely to weigh on the growth of renewable energy, impacting investment and innovation. A loosening of environmental standards for companies is also likely to result in lower demand for anti-pollution chemicals, which is unfavorable for chemical manufacturers.

While the timing and ultimate avenue in which the deregulation agenda progresses is still up for debate, the overall direction is relatively straightforward: This administration is focused on rolling back excessive regulation, with a focus on select sectors. While it may lead to some negative spillovers to specific industries, growth-friendly policies are likely to come into focus for the traditional energy, industrial, and financial sectors.

Style and capitalization impacts

Value vs. Growth: Leveraging company-by-company analysis, both Value and Growth styles are likely to benefit from the deregulatory push. However, though, there are compositional differences depending on the index used.

The share of stocks identified as being impacted the most by deregulation is similar across both the S&P 500 Value and Growth indices, making up approximately 8% of either index. On the other hand, across Russell indices, deregulation exposure is skewed towards Value, with 13.5% of stocks in the Russell 1000 Value index likely to benefit from the deregulatory push versus only 3% in the equivalent Growth index. However, the differences in index classification largely explain the divergence in relative exposure between the S&P and Russell indices, as some industrial stocks—which are poised to benefit from deregulation—are classified as Growth in the S&P but as Value in the Russell.

Source: Jefferis, BofA, Citi, Morgan Stanley, Bloomberg, Principal Asset Management. Data as of May 14, 2025. Note: GICS Sector classification is used; energy related names are included in Industrials sector, environment related names are included in Materials and Healthcare sectors.

Large-cap vs. Small-cap: Utilizing a top-down approach given limited bottom-up small-cap company coverage, findings show that small-cap companies are likely to benefit more from deregulation than large-cap firms. Looking at various sentiment measures, the initial rise in business confidence immediately after the 2024 elections was more pronounced for smaller firms compared to larger firms. This was similar to Trump’s first term, where the surge in NFIB Small Business Optimism Index at the start of 2016 outperformed the CEO Confidence Index. While there has been a decline in sentiment across both measures recently, likely amid a rise in trade uncertainty and potential disappointment that the deregulatory agenda is materializing slower than expected, declines in small business confidence remain more muted compared to the broader market. This likely represents the hope that the deregulatory agenda will eventually materialize, helping support the relative reliance of smaller firms, at least for now.

In terms of profitability, small-cap companies are also likely to enjoy higher margin improvement from regulatory cost savings than large-cap. This is according to data from the National Association of Manufacturers, which indicates that the regulatory compliance costs per employee per year for small companies are 20% higher than those for large companies.

Source: Bloomberg, Principal Asset Management. Data as of March 31, 2025.

Deregulation trade still has room to run

Markets appear to have not yet fully priced in the anticipated effects of deregulation, as reflected in the performance of both sector-level indices and individual stocks expected to benefit. A basket of sectors likely to gain the most—such as energy, financials, and capital goods industrials—continues to trail the relative highs reached in the aftermath of Trump’s election victory last November. The same holds true at the stock level: a group of companies positioned to benefit from deregulation has also underperformed the broader market compared to its initial post-election surge. While many factors influence price performance, these patterns suggest that the deregulation trade remains incomplete.

Among large-cap style indices, the Russell 1000 Value Index appears most sensitive to the deregulation theme and stands to gain the most as additional deregulatory policies are implemented. At the same time, small-cap stocks may benefit even more than large-caps, particularly through improved business confidence and profitability. That said, caution is warranted—index performance will continue to be shaped by a range of other influences, including macroeconomic conditions, liquidity dynamics, and idiosyncratic risks.

Source: Bloomberg, Principal Asset Management. Data as of May 22, 2025. Note: Sector level deregulation beneficiaries performance refers to the average​ return of S&P 500 Energy sector index, Financial index and Capital goods industry index. Stock level deregulation beneficiaries performance refers to the ​average return of all benefited stocks selected by our bottom-up methodology. Broad market benchmark is the S&P 500 index.

Implications for investors

For investors, the slower-than-expected rollout of the Trump administration’s deregulatory agenda has created both uncertainty and opportunity. Early actions—particularly those using the Congressional Review Act—suggest that deregulation will be concentrated in specific sectors, including energy, environment, financials, and healthcare. While the effects will vary across industries, the overall market impact is still likely to be positive. Importantly, markets have yet to fully price in the potential benefits, creating room for upside in select areas for investors positioned ahead of policy implementation.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Salesforce: Don't Overthink This Buying Opportunity
data.description:
Introduction

Since its all time high in early December, Salesforce Inc. (NYSE:CRM) has dropped by approximately 28% despite reporting solid Q1 earnings.

I argue that long-term investors should not worry too much about recently declining growth, with growth likely balancing out and margin expansion already on the way. In addition, CRM is fairly valued to slightly undervalued, marking an attractive entry point.

Growth is slowing down and that is okay

Q1 FY2026 results showed $9.8B in revenue, up 8% YoY. Free Cash flow was strong at $6.3B, up 3.5% YoY. It is important to note that Free Cashflow is historically strong in Q1 as many software contracts are renewed. Data Could and AI ARR grew by 120% YoY, totaling $1B+. This is a very pleasant development. All in all, this quarter was a beat-and-raise.

Still, revenue growth is trending down rapidly and needs to be monitored. Currently, it does seem to be stabilizing around 8%. Therefore, I assume around 8% of top line growth for CRM for the next few years.

Analyst's expectations are likely too high, as $55.3B of revenue in 2028 implies a CAGR of almost 10% from 2024. The company itself has increased their FY2026 guidance from 7.5% to 8.5% growth at the midpoint, respectively, which further supports the thesis of the trend of slowing growth coming to a stop.

Additionally, Salesforce's agentic solution, Agentforce, has the opportunity to revive growth to rates above 8%. Unfortunately, Salesforce does not report specific sales figures for Agentforce. It is included in the Data Cloud and probably makes up a lot of its growth. In Q1, it has reached $100 million in ARR.

The Informatica acquisition should also help boost or at least maintain revenue growth. Salesforce will pay $8B, half in cash half in debt. The stock likely sold off due to this new acquisition considering CRM's poor M&A history. Regarding the amount of free cashflow Salesforce generates as well as the $9B of net debt on their balance sheet, that debt should be of no big concern.

Further, forecasted increases in profitability can offset some slowdowns in revenue growth. I believe it to be likely for Salesforce to become focused on driving profitable growth from here on. They are guiding for a FY2026 operating margin of 21.6%, the highest it has been in company history. Considering the opportunity of AI optimizations to reduce unnecessary expenses alongside scaling their new product pipeline, this goal is not unrealistic whatsoever.

I expect this growth to translate into FCF as well. With a cashflow conversion rate of over 200%, it is clear that many non-cash expenses like stock-based compensation and amortization on acquisitions compress net income. Over the long term, though, I believe that free cash flow is at least as important as net income, if not more. Historically, FCF growth has been very impressive with a 10 year CAGR of 36%. I also like that management makes sure FCF is put to good use, repurchasing a lot of shares, with around $3.1B in capital returns just this quarter. Share count started decreasing in 2022, further boosting FCF/share and EPS, a trend I expect to continue. Dividends are taking an increasing amount of importance too, which could attract some payout-focused investors.

Valuation

On an earnings basis, CRM looks very expensive, trading at a P/E north of 40X although this is the case due to the aforementioned FCF conversion.

On a FCF basis, which I argue is more important for Salesforce, they trade at a yield of 5% (4% when adjusted for stock-based compensation), which I would consider fair for a company with decent growth prospects. A 5% yield is equivalent to a 20X P/FCF ratio, which is around the lowest level the company has traded at since 2008. Slowing valuation levels as a company matures and growth slows are completely normal and in fact necessary. Nevertheless, these low levels do offer a decent margin of safety for new investors.

On this metric, they are also much cheaper than many of their peers, including SAP SE (SAP), Oracle Corp. (ORCL), Adobe Inc. (ADBE), and Microsoft Inc. (MSFT). These companies all offer similar products to Salesforce. As depicted below, only Adobe trades more cheaply on a P/FCF basis, sitting at 20X. All other three competitors are more than double as expensive as CRM even after factoring in Salesforce's high degrees of SBC, despite not having twice as good of growth outlooks.

On a forward P/S ratio, CRM is actually the cheapest amongst their peer group. The second cheapest competitor, once again Adobe, is still 21% more expensive than CRM, trading at 7.5X and 6.2X, respectively.

These asymmetries can be interpreted either as the market discounting Salesforce's future growth more heavily than its competitors or not fully trusting CRM in increasing profitability. Both of these instances represent an opportunity to open a first position (or buy more shares) in Salesforce, as I believe it is fairly valued to slightly undervalued.

Current technicals also support long-term growth. With monthly RSI at 50 and price below the 24 EMA, CRM is trading at levels that often presented itself as an attractive buying opportunity in the past and has technical support to aid a long-term uptrend.

Let's now get to a price target. If revenue grows at 8% for the next five years and free cashflow conversion stays the same with slight profitability gains, FCF could grow at 10% per year, reaching $20B in 2029. As the market realizes that growth is not over for CRM, it re-rates it with a P/FCF multiple of 24, which is still low but slightly higher than today. This would give CRM a market cap of $480B, implying a 13.6% CAGR from now. While not huge, this is likely going to be a market beating return and therefore attractive for me.

Risks

While offering a lot of opportunities for Salesforce, developments in AI could also become a risk. First, I do see the possibility of Agentforce simply not being able to meet current expectations, leading to lower valuations. Arguably more important, Salesforce's approach of seat-based (headcount-based) billing when it comes to software licenses might pressure future revenue growth when AI integration leads to layoffs in a lot of companies due to AI being more efficient than employees in certain fields or tasks.

Another risk for CRM, in addition to slow revenue growth, is relying on acquisitions. Especially when it comes to Slack, CRM paid a huge sales multiple for acquiring them which makes shareholder value creation difficult. If they want to provide solid returns to shareholders then they need to practice better M&A or focus on growing organically.

Conclusion

Quality companies always come at a price. In this case, the market's woes about CRM's future growth are likely overrated, especially on a longer-term horizon. Salesforce grows at decent rates and still has solid future growth potential, especially with Agentforce. Their healthy balance sheet and disciplined capital return policy adds attractiveness. All of the above makes me conclude that Salesforce is a "When in doubt, zoom out" type of company operating in an attractive market and the current dip presents a good buying opportunity.

data.title: Alaska Air Group: Still A Post-Merger Sell With Continued Headwinds
data.description:
Summary

The airline industry is currently traversing through a recession, with waning consumer confidence and a desire to trade down on travel. At the same time, Alaska Air Group, Inc.'s (NYSE:ALK) continued merger integration of Hawaiian Airlines will drag on earnings for at least the next six to twelve months, creating a short opportunity. Through the first quarter, revenues increased, yet so did losses, leading investors to believe expenses will remain elevated, as maintenance, labor, and macroeconomic factors weigh on the shares. Investing in airlines is a complex venture, and ALK share price appreciation will be muted, at best.

First Quarter 2025: Revenues Up, Losses Increase

Alaska Airlines recently reported first quarter 2025 results, which included a miss on top line revenues, yet higher sales growth year over year. The $3.1 billion revenue line item was 41% higher than Q1 2024, as the Hawaiian Airlines acquisition padded sales. However, ALK operated at a $197 million loss for the first three months of 2025, even worse than the $166 million loss in Q1 2024. Higher sales growth and wider losses generally imply two causalities: large expenses, or industry slowdown. We shall examine each of these in turn.

First, let’s discuss where the expenses have ballooned. Below is a snapshot of ALK’s statement of operations per the Q1 press release, with year-over-year comparisons. It shows both the tremendous growth in revenues, as well as where ALK spending was excessive:

Typically, one would expect expenses to increase alongside sales; thus, I consider much of the information unremarkable. However, two line items of note are “Aircraft maintenance” and “Special items – operating”. Recall that the merger with Hawaiian was consummated circa September 2024. The above data, then, only includes Hawaiian financials for the first quarter of 2025, making the 80% increase in maintenance and the 168% increase in special items even more conspicuous. The special items increase was the result of labor contracts, fleet retirement, and HA integration costs. Consider a small snippet from the Q1 press release:

“Labor and other: Labor and other costs in 2025 were primarily for changes to Alaska flight attendants’ sick leave benefits pursuant to a new collective bargaining agreement ratified in the first quarter. Costs in 2024 were primarily associated with the retirement of Alaska’s Airbus and Horizon’s Q400 aircraft.Integration costs: Integration costs are associated with the acquisition of Hawaiian Airlines and primarily consist of employee-related and other merger costs.”

On the face of it, I consider the costs fairly typical, in that there are employees who will be let go post-merger and must be paid compensation. Nevertheless, seven months ago, I observed two distinct risks to ALK’s future performance, in relation to their merger with Hawaiian. The first was the Maui Wildfires of 2023, and the second was additional capital expenditures to replace the HA fleet. Both of these risks made the Hawaiian acquisition expensive, and risky:

“Two significant events occurred. First, the Maui wildfires occurred […] In part, Hawaiian Airlines was severely affected, due to the dangerous conditions, lack of travel, and cancelled tourism. […] Second, the Long-Term Plan presented by Hawaiian Management included additional capital expenditures, specifically related to replacing jet engines in the fleet. These events were dually named “External Factors” in the proxy.”

The Maui wildfires are in the past, but the additional capex was indeed evident in the first quarter, with ALK management spending over $100 million on “Aircraft and aircraft purchase deposits” (per the most recent 10-Q). I will take the thesis one step further, and presuppose additional capex, merger integration costs, and aircraft maintenance throughout fiscal 2025 and into 2026. These company-specific expenses coupled with industry headwinds make Alaska Airlines a decent short candidate over the medium term.

Overarching Macroeconomic Headwinds

There has been news of softness across the airline industry, most recently from a poignant Bloomberg interview with Southwest CEO Bob Jordan. The CEO flatly stated the airline industry is already in a recession, due to labor & maintenance costs, as well as slower bookings on the part of an uncertain consumer. Granted, the interview was in late April at the height of tariff & trade turmoil, but the slowdown in travel has been echoed by companies across the sector. A few examples include Airbnb’s “moderated” Q2 bookings, a “perfect storm” for cheaper flights this summer, and hotel bookings that are flat year over year. Moreover, Silver Airlines’ sudden bankruptcy announcement has rattled investors, lending credence to the dampened mood that comes with an airline recession.

It is a bit difficult to pin down consumer behavior, but it looks as though people are swapping out leisure consumption for other household needs…or at least in the case of flights, postponing plans altogether. In reference to the perfect storm of cheaper flights, there is a cross-current between lower oil prices and price elasticity that is working against Alaska Airlines and its peers. Fuel cost per gallon for ALK was down to $2.61 as of the first quarter, a 13% decrease from Q4 2024; under the usual circumstances, lower prices would translate to higher margins for airlines, but it hasn’t happened. Consumers have been quick to adjust, and wait for lower prices. Given the evidence discussed, the trend of “wait and see” could last at least the next six to twelve months, as leisure spend across the country continues to fizzle. A May 2025 consumer report from S&P Global appears to reinforce the lagging leisure sentiment:

“Heightened macroeconomic uncertainty is likely to remain. Trade conflicts and market volatility may continue to weigh on consumer and business sentiment, and travel and entertainment spending decisions can be delayed or cancelled. This is already hurting hotel demand, particularly at the low end.”

The investor conclusion to draw is that airline investing resembles a tightly coupled, complex system. Much like an air traffic control tower, a multiplicity of safeguards and node checks are necessary to make the system work. But if one or two errors occur, the results could be bleak. Alaska Airlines requires higher revenue per seat mile, higher average capacity, lower fuel costs, and lower labor expenses in order to drive ALK share price appreciation. In my opinion, while ALK is operating efficiently on some fronts, its expenses and unit revenues will be a drag over the medium term. The combination of both macroeconomic headwinds and the continued Hawaiian Airlines integration expense makes ALK a sell.

Valuation & Risks to a Short

Alaska Airlines rates very well versus its peers when it comes to valuation. For example, at $50/share it trades at ~6x forward EV/EBITDA, versus 11.5x as the sector median, according to Seeking Alpha. Below is a chart of ALK forward EV/EBITDA against a basket of peers that includes American Airlines Group Inc. (AAL), Air Canada (OTCQX:ACDVF), SkyWest, Inc. (SKYW), Delta Air Lines, Inc. (DAL), United Airlines Holdings, Inc. (UAL), and JetBlue Airways Corporation (JBLU):

Over the past year, valuations have not changed much, and it seems that ALK is right in the mix. That said, on a relative value basis, I admit that ALK is no better a short candidate than any other airline in the space, save for perhaps JetBlue. Even still, recall both the business-specific nature of expenses that I believe ALK will face over the next 12 months as well as the fragile macroeconomic environment for the industry: I am taking the leap and stating ALK’s valuation multiple will contract, leading to a price of about $30/share. One could argue that given the chart above, JetBlue is on a similar trajectory (i.e., it trades at a high multiple, but there is a risk of a price drop due to lower earnings).

Primary risks to a short include the usual portfolio management issues, such as short squeezes and time. There is a notable 5.7% short interest in the stock, which is not comparatively high (AAL and JBLU carry 10% and 18% short interest, respectively); therefore, there is less risk for a squeeze. Time is also a factor with any shortfall, due to prevailing borrow rates, as well as the general success of the business.

On the first quarter call, management stated, “Integration synergies are tracking slightly ahead of plan […], and our Hawaiian assets are performing well”. Simply stated, if you take management at their word, then I am wrong in the short thesis. That is to say, the risks to a short also include the level of execution at the company. It could very well be that HA will grow, and add to ALK’s bottom line (it is, after all, the reason for the merger). On a similar note, while the industry faces current headwinds, premier leisure is a bright spot, that falls right under Hawaiian’s umbrella. People are still paying for expensive trips, and paying up for the front seats on the plane. One last quote from the conference call, this time by the Chief Revenue Officer Andrew Harrison. The excerpt is in response to a question about Hawaii being a luxury destination:

“The Hawaii franchise and the sort of 25% mainland of our ASMs is actually sort of bucking the trend. The neighbor islands are up double-digit unit revenues. The international franchise in Hawaii actually margins improved 15 points and the mainland with all the connections, the banking, the selling of each other’s metal has just been performing very well, and we’re actually seeing positive unit revenues in that regard.”

Indeed, management execution at Alaska Airlines seems to be going well. As far as a short position, I admit that the next three to six months will be crucial for ALK, and the industry as a whole.

Conclusion

Over the past year, Alaska Airlines shares have traded from a high of $75 down to the current price of $50, and there is still room to run on the short side. The Hawaiian integration will continue to drag on earnings, despite management’s praise for quick synergies and premium travel. In addition, there are key indicators of an airline-specific recession that include consumer preferences, and unit revenues that airlines must lower prices in order to improve capacity. While the macroeconomics of airlines will remain complex and uncertain, Alaska’s expenses will be elevated over the next six to twelve months. The combination will result in muted performance for ALK.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: ATN International: Undervalued Despite Competitive Advantage
data.description:
ATN International Overview

I am initiating coverage on ATN International (NASDAQ:ATNI). ATN International is a telecommunications provider focused on underserved markets like Bermuda, the Cayman Islands, and the Western US and Alaska. They offer services including fixed access, mobile, and business solutions. A key part of their strategy is leveraging government subsidies for underserved areas, providing revenue stability for the business.

The business has had a challenging run over the past year with the stock down more than 27%.

When I first started reviewing the business I didn't have high hopes. Slow growing business, transitioning away from legacy products, and a relatively small scale. While all of that is true, I found the business to be so materially undervalued relative to even a conservative cash flow valuation, that it is deep value opportunity, in addition to a great dividend stock.

ATN International does not need significant growth, or even market growth to justify significant upside and maintain the dividend. In addition, the capital structure is a true competitive advantage relative to comparable companies.

At a price target of $27.64, 70% upside from today's pricing, I rate ATN International a strong buy.

ATNI Stock Valuation

I ran a DCF analysis to generate a price target, nearly always effective in telecom with mature, high-cash-flow companies. Starting with management guidance, expectations were revenue and EBITDA performance in line with 2024 (which was a down year) and lower capital intensity. Given that management has historically been reasonable with guidance, and that guidance is below market growth in all operating regions, I felt this was fair.

Beyond assuming the mid-point of management guidance, I made the following overall assumptions:

  • Revenue growth in out years at 50% of market growth (based on Horizon, Grandview Research which is cited in the following section)
  • Discount rate of 10% based on estimated WACC of 9.5%
  • Long-run growth-rate of 1%, with cost growth offsetting the majority of revenue growth

This DCF-analysis yields a price target of $27.64, 70% upside from today's pricing.

Wall Street analysts actually landing a similar range at an average price target of $29.00, 78% upside from today.

Now the quant is a different story with a sell rating. What is key to me is that Valuation receives an A rating and growth and profitability are fine. The stock has been beaten down this past year after management announced 2024 revenue would decline as some government programs wound down. But that does not affect the forward-looking value, so with an A rating in valuation supporting the findings from my DCF-analysis I am comfortable overlooking the quant rating.

Slow And Steady Growth Is Good Enough

Usually slow and steady growth would not be good enough in telecom, where dividend yields drive outsized valuations and capital intensity risks cash flow. But with the low valuation in this case, its more than enough.

The overall North American market within which ATN operates is expected to grow at 6.2% through 2030.

I assumed a modest 4% growth rate in my DCF and even included 2024's underperformance; ATN's run-rate in the US is over 10%.

The overall Caribbean/Latin America market within which ATN operates is expected to grow at 4.1% through 2030.

I assumed a modest 2% growth rate in my DCF, but ATN has been delivering over 3% growth in revenue, and more from a profitability standpoint.

The slow and steady growth ATN has demonstrated, even if well below market, more than delivers the upside on this stock with additional room for upside if they can outperform my 4% North America and 2% Latin America assumptions.

Shared Capital Spending Model Drives Values

Telecom is a notoriously high capital business due to the infrastructure and construction needed to start service, in addition to ongoing refurbishment and upgrading. Capital intensity from 2016 thru 2022 averaged upwards of 20% of revenue across more than 100 companies studied.

With a capital intensity of 10 to 15% of revenue, even working in less populated regions, ATN has built a major competitive advantage. Using strategic investing in the network alongside government grants, they have been able to drive capital down well below average, reinvesting the cash in shareholders and debt payoffs.

This lower capital intensity provides enhanced cash flow relative to peers and provides an additional level of dividend stability for shareholders.

Downside Risk

As called out in the Q1 earnings call, the two biggest risks to the business are tariff risks and uncertainty around the BEAD Program.

For tariffs, ATN has a heavy sourcing exposure to China, Taiwan, and Vietnam and is expecting some cost pressure or shortages in supply chain. They are able to absorb 2025 impact in current guidance, but continued issues could add pressure to 2026 impacting EBITDA and cash flow, as well as capital expense.

The BEAD Program stands for Broadband Equity, Access, and Deployment. This is a federal program designed to expand high-speed internet access, particularly in unserved and underserved areas which is core to ATN's business model. With recent instability at the federal level, ATN has warned that monetization of their broadband/fiber expansion could be pushed back to 2027.

Both of these risks are largely mitigated by the amount of headroom between current pricing and what I believe is the fair price target. Still, I will be keeping a close eye on these two topics and their impact on ATN.

Verdict

ATN International has had a challenging year with the stock price getting pummeled following revenue losses in the US business as government programs wound down.

Despite that, this is a great value opportunity as the stock is materially undervalued. ATN has a strong competitive advantage in the way they manage their capital program, supporting a healthy dividend. In addition, the business needs only minimal growth, half of market growth in fact.

At a price target of $27.64, 70% upside from today's pricing, I rate ATN International a strong buy.

data.title: AbCellera Biologics: The Dilemma Of A Promising Pipeline Alongside Cash Burn
data.description:
Investment Thesis

I grant AbCellera Biologics Inc. (NASDAQ:ABCL) a Hold rating. In my opinion, the critical aspect of the company is whether it will have sufficient financial resources until at least one of its agreements with other firms, or its drug development program, yields results, assuming that these agreements or programs are successful.

The two proprietary drug programs that AbCellera is developing—namely, ABCL635 and ABCL575—have reached Phase 1 of clinical trials. These drug candidates will require several years to be commercialized if the company successfully develops them, in the best scenario. Additionally, none of the agreements that AbCellera has with other firms to develop new medical drugs have reached Phase 3 of clinical trials. Consequently, if these collaborations are successfully commercialized, it will require time for AbCellara to receive royalties from these agreements.

The company has burned significant resources. It reported net losses of $163 million in 2024. In the same period, it held cash, cash equivalents, and marketable securities of $626 million, which are the main financial resources to fund its operations. These resources would last less than four years at the rate at which the company has burned them. Ultimately, when pondering all the negative and positive aspects of AbCellera, I rate the company with a hold buy rating.

AbCellera Biologics Financial Analysis

I would like to start this analysis by showing AbCellera's financial position. The analysis will reveal where the company stands without introducing bias. With that being said, the following chart illustrates AbCellera's revenues and operating expenses between 2019 and 2024. It reveals that in 2020-2022, the company reported significant revenues, and then its revenues experienced a sharp decline. Due to this behavior, the company reported significant losses, since its operating expenses have consistently increased over time.

To answer why the revenues plunged in 2023, it is necessary to analyze the company's segments. The chart below reveals that royalty revenues were the catalyst for the company to achieve excellent financial results between 2020 and 2022. However, royalty revenues dropped to zero in 2023 and have remained at that level since that year. The chart also indicates that research fees, licensing revenues, and milestone payments have a small contribution to the revenues. In other words, what really matters to the company is obtaining royalty revenues.

At this point, an analysis of the company's business model is helpful in understanding how the revenues are generated. AbCellera focuses on discovering and developing drug candidates on behalf of other firms. In other words, the company concentrates its operations on Phase 1 of the drug development process. In this phase, companies develop drug candidates. Subsequently, these candidates are tested in the preclinical phase, or Phase 2, on animals or in vitro. If the drug candidate is successful in Phases 1 and 2, it will proceed to the other phases of the drug development process. Turning to AbCellera, the company only concentrates its activities on Phase 1, without involvement in the other steps of clinical research.

AbCellera focuses on antibody drug candidates. Antibodies are proteins that the immune system uses to protect the body against antigens ((bacteria, viruses, venom, etc.)). The company has developed a technology to create drug candidates in the areas of G-protein-coupled receptors ((GPCRs)) and ion channels, and T-cell engagers ((TCEs)).

Returning to the point of how the company obtains its revenues, AbCellera generates revenues by stabilizing partnerships with biopharmaceutical companies, pharmaceutical companies, non-profit institutions, and government institutions. These agreements stipulate that AbCellera is in charge of developing drug candidates ((Phase 1)), and its counterparts will conduct the other steps of the drug development process using those candidates. The cooperation contemplates that AbCellera receives fee payments to develop the drug candidate.

In addition, the company receives milestone payments, commercial payments, and royalties depending on the success of the drug candidates. As commented on before in this section, most of the company's revenues come from royalties. The royalties are generated if the drug candidate successfully passes all phases of drug development and is commercialized. These royalties are a percentage of the drug's sales.

Risk: Time

From my perspective, the business model of AbCellera encapsulates a significant risk. For example, an oil company uses a drill to bore a hole. If it finds oil, it can commercialize this product. Drilling typically takes 40-50 days. Therefore, the worst that can occur to an oil company is to lose 40-50 days drilling a well and not finding oil. Turning to AbCellera, the firm could wait years, and even for more than a decade, until its drug development agreements bear significant fruit. Alternatively, the drug trial could implode.

On average, it takes from 10 to 15 years to develop a new medicine. Therefore, the drug candidates that the company develops this year would yield revenues years later, assuming that they pass all phases of the drug development process.

Liquidity and COVID-19

In December 2020, AbCellera started to quote in the financial market. In that year, COVID-19 was a serious threat to society. Consequently, Governments and private companies invested significant resources to combat the pandemic. Due to this reason, in 2020-2021, the biopharma industry experienced a substantial influx of resources. In this context, Bamlanivimab ((LY-CoV555)), a neutralizing monoclonal antibody medicine designed for treating patients with mild to moderate COVID-19 symptoms, received an emergency use authorization from the FDA. This drug was a joint cooperation between AbCellera and Lilly (LLY). Due to that medication and the Bebtelovimab ((LY-CoV1404)) medical treatment, AbCellera obtained significant royalty revenues between 2020 and 2022. However, in 2022, the FDA stopped the commercialization of Bebtelovimab, as it does not neutralize the COVID-19 subvariants Omicron BQ.1 and BQ.1.1. This decision eroded AbCellera's revenues, as indicated in the Financial Analysis section.

Under this scenario, the logic should indicate that AbCellera would have moderated its operating expenses. However, they have increased by 345.52% between 2020 and 2024, reaching $344 million in the last fiscal year. The following chart reveals that AbCellera's total cash, cash equivalents, and marketable securities declined between 2021 and 2024. They dropped by $261 million in that period. These liquid assets are the primary resources that the company has for a cash runway to finance its operations. If the company continues to burn these liquid resources at that rate, it will run out of money in less than three years.

Partnership Program

The following table shows the drug candidates that have reached the clinical stage. These medical treatments are those in which AbCellera has cooperation agreements with other firms. I exclude from the list those medicines that do not expect further development. In total, there are 11 drug candidates. From these potential medical treatments, five drug candidates have been paused. In this section, I discuss some of these arrangements.

The ABD-147 drug candidate is in Phase 1. This experimental drug is a radiopharmaceutical biological therapy that is used to treat neuroendocrine carcinoma. To provide context, the global market size for neuroendocrine tumor treatment was $2.71 billion in 2024. Meanwhile, the IVX-01 studies an undisclosed chronic disease in dogs. As such, it is not possible to get insight into the potential market size of this therapy. Moving to AB-2100, this drug candidate intends to treat the clear cell renal cell carcinoma ((ccRCC)), which is the most common type of kidney cancer. The market size of ccRCC was estimated at $4.3 billion in 2023. The other drug candidates in the table are paused.

Among the 97 drug collaboration agreements from which the company could earn royalties if the medical treatments are successfully developed, the average royalty rate established in these agreements is 3.2%. I use this percentage to estimate AbCellera's potential revenues if the ABD-147 and AB-2100 candidates are commercialized. The total would be:

  • [$2,700 million + $4,300 million] x 3.2% = $224 million.

This estimation assumes that the two drugs are the only ones commercialized in those markets. In the previous table, there are 11 medical treatments under development. Consequently, if the company develops both drugs, it will have an 18.18% probability of success. To provide context of how these resources may potentially help AbCellera, the $224 million will cover the $163 million net losses reported by the company in 2024.

Internal Program

The company has its own internal drug development program. It is conducting the development of two medical treatments, namely, ABCL635 and ABCL575. For this purpose, AbCellera uses its own resources to finance both medical therapies. On May 14, 2025, the company announced that it had received authorization from Health Canada to proceed to Phase 1 of clinical trials ((ABCL635 therapy)). In addition, on May 30 of the same year, AbCellera also received positive news. Health Canada authorized the ABCL575 drug candidate to advance to clinical trials ((Phase 1)). Due to these announcements, AbCellera's stock price increased by 53.66%, as depicted in the following chart.

The ABCL635 program intends to address vasomotor symptoms, or hot flashes, which are associated with menopause. Approximately 30% of women experience moderate to severe vasomotor symptoms when they face menopause. These symptoms can alter sleep, concentration, energy, mood, etc. According to the company, the market size of this drug is more than $2 billion. To grant context, AbCellera reported revenues of $28.83 million in 2023. Therefore, its revenues could increase considerably if this drug candidate is successfully developed.

On the other hand, the ABCL575 is a potential antibody drug for the treatment of atopic dermatitis. The target has the potential to treat a broad range of inflammatory conditions and autoimmune diseases, such as colitis/inflammatory bowel disease, asthma/atopy, and diabetes. AbCellera indicates that the atopic dermatitis market size is $11 billion. This figure is 382 times AbCellera's revenues.

ABSI Stock - Peer Analysis

This section conducts a peer analysis among AbCellera, Regeneron (REGN), Absci (ABSI), Twist Bioscience (TWST), OmniAb Inc (OABI), Evotec (EVO), Charles River (CRL), and Fortrea (FTRE). These companies develop antibody-based medical treatments or collaborate with other firms to develop such therapies. The following table shows the ratios to analyze.

In the last year, AbCellera's stock price declined by 7.89%, considerably lower than the median in the peer group. However, in the last five years, the company experienced a further decline in its stock price compared to the median drop of its peers.

Moving to the valuation ratios, the EV/Sales metrics suggest that AbCellera is considerably overvalued compared to its peers. The Price/Sales ratio, which is 40.28, also indicates that the company presents an overvaluation. However, AbCellera has a price-to-book value of 0.92, suggesting the company is undervalued.

For this year, the company expects an 11.41% growth rate in its revenues, which is higher than the median in the peer group. However, its return on equity of 15.59% points out that the company generates financial losses. All the companies in the peer group, except REGN, have reported negative return on equity. These results suggest that this market is facing considerable losses. On the other hand, AbCellera has the highest current ratio, a positive aspect. It indicates that it has significant resources to continue developing its drug program.

Many companies in the peer group are in the process of developing new drugs. Achieving the commercialization of these products requires time. For this reason, the companies in the peer group have experienced declines in their stock prices and have negative returns on equity, since they have not brought new medical drugs to the market. AbCellera is not the exception. Consequently, I consider that the company has been affected by these headwinds.

Conclusion

In light of the analysis presented in this article, I rate AbCellera with a hold rating. While it is true that the two drug proprietary programs that the company is developing have reached Phase 1 of clinical trials, it is also true that it will require years for these drugs to be commercialized—assuming they are developed successfully. The company has to face significant expenses in the clinical trial phases to develop its two drug candidates.

In my opinion, the high operating expenses that the company faces are its primary threat. In 2024, AbCellera reported operating expenses of $344 million, while its revenues were $29 million in the same year. The company did not cut its operating costs in 2023, even though its royalty revenues, obtained from its participation in the Bamlanivimab medicine, dropped to zero. This participation was its primary source of revenue. Instead of cutting costs, the company has increased its operating expenses.

In the partnership program that AbCellera has with other companies, none of the drug candidates have reached Phase 3 of clinical trials. Therefore, it would require time until these drug candidates can be commercialized if they are successfully developed. In my view, the company will continue to burn money for at least two years. Taking all these factors into consideration, I rate AbCellera with a Hold rating.

data.title: International Consolidated Airlines: Deep Value While Everyone Watches The War
data.description:
Introduction

International Consolidated Airlines Group S.A. (OTCPK:ICAGY) (OTCPK:BABWF) (BMAD:IAG) (LSE:IAG) is not a well-known name in the U.S. investing circle, and most likely, this is the reason for this stock to be so undervalued. It’s an OTC-traded European airline group that owns British Airways, Iberia, Aer Lingus, and Vueling. The stock price has increased by about 100% in the past year. It has strong fundamentals and a surprisingly resilient Q1 performance. And I have to say, the setup looks like it’s just the beginning. This stock dipped today almost 5% on the Middle East news, and as an Israeli, I have to say, I see here the potential for a very quick gain, and I will make this trade.

I’m writing this while sitting in Israel, and honestly, having no idea what happens next. This is what it looks like when macro risk becomes a reality. The only thing I know for sure, everything passes, especially when the news is not so fresh, and in my opinion, by Monday, the market will start bouncing back slowly (if things don’t get out of perspective, of course, but it doesn’t look like it). Besides, conflicts like this typically send airline stocks down, but not all of them deserve to be treated the same way. IAG has something most airlines don’t have. They earn nice money, and this stock, even after rallying 100%, trades at a deep-value multiple. Stocks like this have no business crashing on the news, but the market is moved by the sentiment, so all we have left to do is make money on it.

Company Overview & Q1 Results

The company posted a Q1 operating profit of €198 million, up €130 million year-over-year. Despite disruptions like Heathrow’s one-day closure (which cost British Airways €50M), late Easter timing, and FX headwinds, IAG improved operating margin by 1.7 points to 2.8%.

Revenue rose 9.6% year-over-year to €7 billion, driven by robust transatlantic and Latin American demand. Iberia and British Airways led the profitability gains, with Iberia hitting a 7.5% margin thanks to North and South Atlantic route performance. Vueling (IAG’s low-cost Spanish airline that covers mostly short-distance flights across Europe) saw a slight dip this quarter, but that’s more about the Easter calendar shift than anything else. It’s the most seasonal business in the group, so temporary drops like this aren’t unusual.

According to management, corporate travel is holding up well across all regions, with no major changes so far despite everything going on in the world. And when it comes to passenger demand, the premium cabins are being strong. There’s been some softness in economy bookings from U.S. travelers, which might be due to the macro pressure or just people pulling back a little, but that weakness is being balanced out by strong demand for business and first-class seats.

If we look at the performance by region, the North Atlantic saw the strongest increase in unit revenue of any region year-on-year. Robust performance was evidence across each of the transatlantic airlines, all of them experiencing high single digit or double digit percentage increases in revenue. In particular, premium cabins performed well.

What’s Going On Politically

Right now, geopolitical risk is front and center. Oil is spiking after Israel launched Operation Am Kelevi (Lion Nation), a direct military campaign against Iran’s nuclear infrastructure. That’s obviously not good news for airlines, especially with jet fuel already expected to cost €7.5 billion for IAG this year.

But the funniest thing is that IAG isn’t heavily exposed to the Middle East like the Emirates or Qatar. Of course, it has some exposure, but its core earnings come from transatlantic and European markets, which did not change. Besides, the company has already hedged 65% of its fuel needs for 2025, so the recent spike in oil prices (CL1:COM) isn’t a disaster scenario for them, either. It’ll hurt a bit, sure, but it’s manageable. At the same time, net debt is falling (now down to €6.1 billion), and leverage is already below 1x. In my opinion, this is much more important for this specific stock, than the conflict and the oil prices.

Valuation

ICAGY trades at just 6.41x forward GAAP earnings and EV/EBITDA is only 3.56x. That’s 60–70% lower than sector medians. The group is profitable, with a net income margin close to 9% and a return on equity north of 60%. Is it only me, or is it a running joke that airlines are seldom profitable? From what I always knew about airlines, they are saying that things will turn around this year, and then they will borrow more and more money. This company is not only profitable, but they have growing margins. Totally untypical for this sector.

Risks & Catalysts

The obvious thing I must mention – this is an airline stock. That means it’s never going to be immune to macro pressures, and right now, we have tons of them. The biggest immediate risk is geopolitical. I’m writing this from Israel, where the country just launched a military operation against Iran. Oil spiked over 13% in a day, the biggest jump since Russia invaded Ukraine. And while IAG is well-hedged, as I explained before, it still expects to spend around €7.5 billion on jet fuel this year. If oil stays elevated, or worse, if Iran retaliates in a way that disrupts global supply, fuel costs could pressure margins even with hedging. JPM had actually said that this attack could be the cause of an oil price surge to $120, which sounds exaggerated in my opinion, but who am I to question their prediction?

There’s also the Boeing tragedy. A 787-8 operated by Air India crashed this week, and while IAG’s aircraft weren’t involved, they do fly 787s, and just ordered 71 new wide bodies, many of them from Boeing. That kind of headline doesn’t help investor confidence, especially with safety investigations now underway and Indian regulators reportedly grounding part of the Dreamliner fleet.

Beyond that, demand trends aren’t perfect. Expensive, premium cabins are helping the company right now, but there has been some softness in U.S. economy bookings, which is hard to ignore. Unclear where things will go from here, but if macro stays weak, or if consumers pause traveling, it could definitely hit this company.

That said, there are also some real positives here. The company has already kicked off a major buyback program, which shows confidence from management and usually sends a strong signal to the market. Net debt is going down, margins are moving in the right direction, and they’re still sticking to their plan to grow capacity by around 3% this year, which is ambitious considering the macro backdrop. If things settle down even a little, whether it’s oil prices, market volatility, or geopolitical fears, there’s a good chance ICAGY won’t stay this cheap for long.

And I think it will all calm down sooner than everyone expects. Oil isn’t seeing the biggest demand, and it’s weakening as time passes. OPEC is holding the prices, but the organization has its own difficulties, and if it lets the price go, I can only imagine how quickly it will drop. As for the conflict, I am not a war correspondent, but I have been following the situation here, in Israel, closely enough to understand what is happening.

After today’s attack, it will be difficult for Iran to answer. While we are not aware of the full force of the Iranian military, we know for sure that it was seriously damaged today. As we saw after the Hezbollah and Hamas attacks, people, and commanders are sometimes even more important than the weapons, and when they get unexpectedly taken out, it lowers the chances for successful retribution.

Conclusion

There’s nothing speculative about ICAGY at this point. The company is profitable, actively paying down debt, and growing margins, yet the stock still trades like it’s stuck in the post-COVID meltdown. And yes, the headlines right now are scary about war in the Middle East, oil surging, etc. Investor’s nerves are understandably on edge, it hasn’t been a calm couple of months. But this is exactly the kind of situation where certain stocks get mispriced, not because of their fundamentals, but because they happen to be in the wrong sector at the wrong time. IAG isn’t some fragile budget airline barely hanging on.

International Consolidated Airlines Group S.A. is a well-run, asset-rich group that’s executing on its strategy and returning cash to shareholders. If the market keeps panicking, I’d honestly see that as an opportunity because ICAGY is set to make an epic comeback story that most people haven’t even noticed.

data.title: Tesla's Last Dance: Slumping Deliveries And Political Feud (Downgrade)
data.description:
Last time I wrote about Tesla, Inc. (NASDAQ:TSLA) a month ago, and gave it a “Hold.” Since then, the stock has declined nearly 6% and is down 19% year-to-date due to multiple reasons, including the May sales decline, Goldman Sachs' cut in forecast, and the ongoing conflict between President Trump and CEO Elon Musk. Last time, I said that Tesla stock was gaining only on the heels of Trump and Musk's friendship, as well as Trump's policies favoring Musk’s EV company. As we’ve seen since the election, Tesla stock reached new highs in December 2024, following Trump's win.

But now this relationship is fading as Musk’s harsh public criticism of Trump’s "One Big Beautiful Bill," including calls for impeachment and revealing potentially damning information, has triggered a public feud. However, Musk's recent apology eased some worries, but investors are keeping an eye on the upcoming news.

Beyond political worries, Tesla has its own problems. Now, the reality is that Tesla is feeling sales and delivery slumps on the ground. Goldman Sachs just cut Tesla’s targets, noting U.S. deliveries off mid-teens and Europe down roughly 50%. Goldman now projects Q2 2025 deliveries at just 365,000 units, down from its prior estimate of 410,000 and well below the consensus forecast of 417,000. Sales in the month of May 2025 plunged in major markets (e.g., Germany 36%, UK 45% year-over-year).

In short, demand is weak and competition fierce, despite occasional offers or model refreshes.

The only near-term positives are the planned rollout of robotaxis and updates to the Optimus robot. However, these are still “years away” bets; Musk himself says autonomy/Optimus are long-term drivers. Moreover, Tesla’s head of the Optimus humanoid robot program is set to leave the firm. Tesla gave a June 22 date for the launch of Robotaxi in Austin. Any glow from a demo is unlikely to erase today’s headwinds. I don’t think it will be easy to turn around the recent sell-off, as Tesla’s EV business is in a critical stage.

Moreover, Tesla is trading at nosebleed levels with 145x expected earnings, whereas its peers are trading at decent valuations, signaling to avoid Tesla now. Due to these reasons, including weak demand, I downgrade Tesla to "Sell."

Trump and Musk's Conflict Overview

Recently, the Trump–Musk relationship turned openly hostile. Elon Musk publicly denounced President Trump’s proposed tax-cut bill on X, calling it a “disgusting abomination.” Trump said he’s “very disappointed” in Musk for opposing what he termed the “biggest tax cuts in history,” arguing it would add trillions to the deficit and roll back EV incentives. On Truth Social, Trump called Musk “crazy” and suggested reviewing Musk’s federal subsidies and contracts, potentially cutting off support for Tesla and SpaceX (SPACE). Musk retaliated online, warning that new tariffs “will cause a recession in the second half of this year,” and distanced himself from politics, exiting his Trump administration role in late May to focus more on Tesla and SpaceX.

Just a day ago, Elon Musk publicly apologized to Donald Trump, and Trump accepted the apology, causing a temporary policy ceasefire and a slight recovery in Tesla stock. This reconciliation lowers the policy overhang in the near term and eases some investor concerns about subsidy and contract risk. However, the political risk has not disappeared; the immediate danger of losing EV incentives or having government contracts canceled has subsided somewhat, but long-term policy risk remains until actual legislation shields EV incentives.

A key dispute centers on EV incentives: Trump removed tax credits, prompting criticism from Musk. Reports link this spat to Tesla’s stock swings, with shares plunging double digits at times and wiping over $100 billion in market value. Musk was once a major Trump backer (even the largest GOP donor in 2024), but investors now worry the feud may hurt Tesla. Facing criticism and falling Tesla sales, Musk has cut back on side projects like DOGE.

Investors cheered Musk’s comments that the DOGE project is “mostly done” and that he will refocus on Tesla, viewing this pivot away from crypto and politics as critical to restoring investor trust.

Tesla Delivery Numbers and Competitor Comparison

Tesla deliveries have seen a huge decline in 2025. In Q1 2025, the company reported about 336,681 global vehicle deliveries, a 13% down. China accounted for roughly 40% of that (134,607 units), reflecting Tesla’s second-largest market.

However, Tesla is now trailing its domestic rival. In April 2025, Chinese maker BYD Company Limited (OTCPK:BYDDF) sold 7,231 pure BEVs in Europe, slightly more than Tesla’s 7,165 in the same month. Tesla’s market share in Europe has been declining, as its lineup has aged and lower-priced Chinese models have arrived.

In fact, Tesla’s sales are down sharply in many European countries. A Reuters survey of May 2025 data shows Tesla registrations plunging 30–60% y/y in markets like Sweden, Portugal, and France, even as overall EV registrations are rising.

Norway was a notable bright spot; the refreshed Model Y launch boosted sales there, but otherwise, Tesla’s decline is industry-wide.

By contrast, BYD’s output is surging. BYD reported about 986,098 NEV (battery + plug-in) sales in Q1 2025, including 416,388 BEVs. That gave BYD a ~15.4% share of global BEV sales vs Tesla’s ~12.6%. Other Chinese automakers (SAIC, Geely, etc.) and even startups like Xiaomi are also eating into Tesla’s China and Europe business.

Meanwhile, in the U.S. market, Tesla still leads EV sales by a wide margin, but competition is emerging. Rivian Automotive, Inc. (RIVN), for example, delivered only 8,640 vehicles in Q1 2025. Rivian's Q1 Sales Fell 36% Amid L.A. Wildfire Fallout (down 36% y/y), illustrating the gap between Tesla and smaller players. Tesla has promised new, affordable models and aims to stay #1 worldwide. But current delivery trends show Tesla holding a smaller share of the European and Chinese markets, while BYD, other Chinese brands, and even incumbents like VW gain ground.

Put simply, Tesla’s Q1 output of 337k remains large, but its growth is outpaced by BYD’s 1 million NEVs, and even Tesla’s China sales are flagging. May sales of China-made Model 3/Y fell 15% y/y to 61,662 units. These figures highlight Tesla’s leadership but also its eroding dominance as competition and market shifts intensify.

Tesla’s Profit Struggles and Financial Health

Tesla’s profitability has been under strain. In Q1 2025, Tesla’s automotive revenue fell about 20% year-on-year, and net income plunged 71% compared to Q1 2024.

Automotive gross profit was roughly $1.46 billion, yielding a gross margin of only 11.3%, well below the 15–20% levels of prior years.

Operating margin was near 2.1%, down 3.4 percentage points from a year earlier. These results missed analyst targets and underscored weak demand.

In its Q1 earnings, the company noted that margins were “stronger-than-expected” due to cost cuts, but revenue was still down, and profit collapsed.

Deutsche Bank’s analysts have warned Tesla’s automotive margins have sunk to the lowest on record as it ramps up sales incentives to boost volume. TSLA Q1 Earnings Miss Mark, Investors Pin Hopes on Robotaxi, Cheap EVs. Tesla's affordable EV, robotaxis in focus after Musk backlash, competition hurt demand.

One key headwind for Tesla is the U.S. tax credit policy. New 2024 rules tightened domestic content requirements, causing some Tesla models to lose the $7,500 federal EV tax credit. For example, the Cybertruck AWD and many Model 3/Y units built with non-U.S. batteries became ineligible. But under the 2025 requirements, vehicles must be assembled in North America and have at least 60% of battery components and critical minerals sourced from qualifying U.S. or free-trade countries to claim the full credit. This effectively raised Tesla’s effective prices and may have shaved demand.

J.P. Morgan analysts estimate that the elimination of these credits (and related California incentives) could cut Tesla’s annual profit by roughly $1.2 billion.

Moreover, Trump’s “One Big Beautiful Bill” (and follow-on proposals) would remove all remaining EV incentives, a move that Musk explicitly blamed for their fallout. In short, reduced subsidies mean thinner profit margins on each car and lower volume, pressuring Tesla’s finances.

Finally, Tesla’s balance sheet shows some stress. Tesla’s debt-to-equity ratio was about 0.66, reflecting moderate leverage (debt $49.8 billion vs. equity $75.4 billion).

Tesla’s free cash flow narrowed significantly in Q1 2025, falling to about $664 million from nearly $2.8 billion in Q4 2024. The decline comes as the company ramps up investments in new factories and products.

Put together, the low margins, disappearing subsidies, modest net cash flow, and rising tariffs have sapped Tesla’s profitability. These financial indicators, steep profit declines, lower margins, credit losses, and weaker cash flow, highlight the financial stress Tesla faced in 2025 and why its investors have grown uneasy about its near-term outlook.

Valuation Analysis

While looking at Tesla's valuation, the multiples look extreme. Its forward P/E is 145× and P/S is 15×, dwarfing peers.

BYD, by comparison, trades around 20 to 25× forward P/E and 1.4× P/S. The broader auto and EV sector is much cheaper. For example, the auto industry's median P/S is around 3 times and P/E is at 16.7 times. Even luxury automakers typically trade below 5×. In short, Tesla’s valuation is roughly 3× higher on a revenue basis than a typical EV maker.

Compared to another peer, Toyota Motor Corporation (TM), which trades at a 0.70 Forward sales ratio, indicates that the company is significantly undervalued compared to Tesla and also the sector median of 0.90x.

Analysts expect Tesla to grow sales by about 17 to 18% in 2025, reaching approximately $117 billion. At the current stock price of around $300, a 155× P/E implies only $2 EPS next year, meaning Tesla’s valuation already assumes massive profit growth. To justify a 9.8× P/S ratio (compared to the 3× sector median), Tesla would need to roughly triple its earnings, or the stock would need to fall by about two-thirds.

For instance, increasing non-GAAP EPS from $2 to $6 would bring the forward P/E closer to 50× and the P/S ratio in line with peers.

Even with generous assumptions, Tesla’s fair value is much lower than current levels. A 50× multiple on $3 to $4 EPS points to a fair share price of around $150. Using a 3× P/S on $117 billion in revenue suggests a market cap of about $350 billion, or $100 per share. This implies Tesla is significantly overvalued unless it delivers much faster earnings growth.

Risks to My Thesis

Bulls often say the game is not yet over for Tesla as it has some catalysts in the face of Robotaxi and Optimus. Tesla has signaled a mid-2025 launch for its autonomous Robotaxi service in Austin (recent reports target June 22, 2025, with volume production of the purpose-built Cybercab slated for 2026. Realised, the Robotaxi fleet would open a large new ride-hailing revenue stream and reinforce Tesla’s lead in full autonomy. The Cybercab’s minimalist “unboxed” design also promises sharply lower production costs, potentially boosting margins on high-utilisation vehicles.

Tesla’s Optimus humanoid robot is on a slower track. The Q1 2025 shareholder deck states Optimus “builds on our Fremont pilot line in 2025.” Musk has since indicated low-volume production for factory use in 2025, scaling up (and selling externally) by 2026.

Strategically, deploying Optimus in Tesla factories could cut labor costs and improve efficiency in handling repetitive tasks in logistics/manufacturing while positioning Tesla in a nascent robotics market. Over time, Optimus sales might also diversify Tesla’s business, but material revenue is likely beyond the next year.

Conclusion

For me, Tesla is in a critical situation where it's currently facing plenty of challenges, including political headwinds and financial issues. However, the success of robotaxi could act as a catalyst for the company, but it will not be easy, as Tesla must navigate complex regulations, prove safety and reliability, and scale the service amid fierce competition.

The recent Musk–Tesla reconciliation provides a short-lived policy reprieve, easing some subsidy and contract fears, but underlying demand challenges and stretched multiples persist. At about 145× forward earnings, Tesla’s valuation reflects sky-high expectations; there’s virtually no room for error without risking a steep correction.

Any misstep, slower deliveries, margin misses, or delays in robotaxi/Optimus could spark a significant downside. I see little upside from here over the next few months and maintain a Sell recommendation until more tangible catalysts emerge.
